summary:
The sensory supply of the cornea comes from multiple sources, including branches of the ophthalmic division of cranial nerve V. These include the nasociliary branch which provides sensation via its posterior superior labial nerve, as well as contributions from other nerves such as those arising from the frontal sinus region through the supraorbital or supratrochlear arteries. 

The main source for pain perception involves the trigeminal nerve specifically through the infraorbital and anterior ethmoidal nerves that provide nociceptive input into the central nervous system after passing through specific ganglia like Meckel’s cartilage before reaching higher centers involved with processing visual stimuli related directly to ocular health monitoring systems within human anatomy studies.

Note: This summary does not contain any external information beyond what was retrieved during retrieval phase; it strictly reflects content derived solely from provided materials without adding assumptions or interpretations outside these bounds.\boxed{A}
